title: Fact-Checking Workflow
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive decision-tree workflow guiding students through the SIFT method to systematically evaluate environmental claims

learningObjectives: Execute a systematic fact-checking workflow (SIFT) when presented with an environmental claim
Evaluate source credibility by examining funding, credentials, and publication history
Distinguish between reliable, uncertain, misleading, and false environmental claims using evidence
prerequisites: Basic understanding of scientific evidence and peer review
Familiarity with the concept of media bias

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Guided decision-tree workflow with expert comparison
keyQuestions: How can you tell if a source is credible when evaluating an environmental claim?
Why is it important to check multiple sources rather than relying on one?
What is the difference between a misleading claim and a false claim?
How does tracing to the original source change your assessment?
What patterns do you notice among claims that turn out to be misleading?
commonMisconceptions: Students often judge claims by whether they 'sound scientific' rather than by checking sources
Students may assume professional-looking websites are always credible
Students may think fact-checking means simply Googling the claim rather than following a systematic process
assessmentOpportunities: Students record their process and findings for at least 4 claims in a structured table
Students apply the SIFT method to a real environmental claim found independently
Students compare their assessments to expert evaluations and reflect on discrepancies
